package.executiveFraming: Bethany House’s staffing transition should be treated as a service-continuity and relationship-continuity problem, not only as a question of adding hours or roles. The current decision frame is: continuity matters during staffing transition; implementation needs an owner; sequence should protect service delivery while adding capacity.
package.recommendationSummary:
Recommended position: Phased relationship handoff — Add capacity in stages while explicitly transferring partner history and decision ownership.

This is the leading option in the current weighted comparison. It ranks above Centralized transition coordinator with clear escalations because its counterevidence burden is lower across the admitted criteria, especially on partner continuity during the staffing transition.
package.recommendationRationale:
The main reason to recommend a phased relationship handoff is that it directly matches Bethany House’s stated need to preserve partner continuity during the staffing transition while still adding capacity. Bethany House confirmed that partner continuity matters, and the sequence should protect service delivery while capacity is added. A staged handoff is the option most directly built around that sequence: it does not simply assign a new role or split functions; it requires partner history and decision ownership to move deliberately from the current holder to the next accountable person.

The recommendation should not be read as risk-free. A staged handoff can become ceremonial if it transfers names without transferring context, norms, unresolved commitments, trust, and decision rights. It can also fail if the original relationship holder remains the de facto escalation point, preventing real capacity gain. The option also does not, by itself, name one accountable owner for the whole implementation or for failed handoffs. That gap matters because Bethany House also identified the need for a clear implementation owner.

For that reason, the recommendation should include an explicit implementation condition: before the handoff begins, Bethany House should name who owns the transition, who has authority to resolve failed handoffs, and when unresolved issues escalate. Without that owner, the selected position loses one of the requirements in the decision frame.
package.currentPositionStatement:
The current position is to proceed with a phased relationship handoff: add capacity in stages while explicitly transferring partner history and decision ownership. This position should protect service delivery by sequencing the transition rather than moving all relationships or responsibilities at once. It should also make the invisible parts of partner relationships visible enough to transfer: history, expectations, norms, open commitments, decision rights, and escalation paths.

The decision should be reopened if any of the following tripwires appear: partner history is not documented before transfer; the current relationship holder remains the default escalation point after transfer; no single implementation owner is named; failed handoffs sit unresolved between roles; or the staged sequence moves too slowly for the staffing transition timeline.

candidateCommentary.rationale: Phased relationship handoff is the recommended position because it best fits the current frame that partner continuity matters during the staffing transition and that service delivery should be protected while capacity is added. Its strongest fit is continuity: it directly addresses the risk that staffing pressure is partly a relationship-continuity problem. Its main weakness is implementation accountability. The description includes transfer of decision ownership, but Bethany House would still need a named owner for the whole transition and a repair path for failed handoffs.
candidateCommentary.comparisonReason: It ranks above Centralized transition coordinator with clear escalations because the current evidence against it is less burdensome across the weighted criteria, particularly on partner continuity.

candidateCommentary.rationale: Centralized transition coordinator with clear escalations remains a live option. It is the clearest direct answer to the need for one implementation owner and failed-handoff accountability. It could help Bethany House track dependencies, coordinate sequencing, and escalate unresolved issues. Its main limitation is that coordination is not the same as relationship transfer. Partner continuity may still depend on original relationship holders unless partner history and trust are deliberately handed off.
candidateCommentary.comparisonReason: It ranks second. It is stronger than the selected option on clear implementation ownership, but its material risks on partner continuity and bottlenecking keep it behind the phased handoff in the current comparison.

candidateCommentary.rationale: Separate operational and people ownership also remains live. It addresses the broader concern that too much responsibility may sit with one person and recognizes that people-system work and executive coordination may require different forms of accountability. It is supported by Bethany House’s recent HR-related work, including compensation analysis, salary updates where needed, and hiring an HR firm for payroll and compliance. Its weakness is that it does not directly solve partner continuity unless relationship ownership is explicitly assigned and transferred.
candidateCommentary.comparisonReason: It ranks third in the current comparison because it carries material risks across all three admitted criteria: partner continuity is indirect, failed-handoff accountability can become ambiguous across seams, and service delivery may face added coordination burden during transition.

package.closingNote: The recommendation is therefore to use phased relationship handoff as the organizing approach, while borrowing one discipline from the centralized coordinator option: name a real implementation owner and escalation path before the first handoff begins. The decision should stay open to revision if new Bethany House evidence shows that ownership clarity is more urgent than relationship transfer, or if early handoffs show that continuity is not actually being preserved.

package.executiveFraming: Bethany House’s current decision frame centers on continuity with long-standing partners, explicit ownership during transition, and visibility into transition costs. Within that frame, the recommended position is: “Phased relationship handoff — Add capacity in stages while explicitly transferring partner history and decision ownership.”
package.recommendationSummary: The selected position is the comparison leader. It ranks ahead of “Separate operational and people ownership” and “Relationship continuity buffer” because it most directly addresses the stated risk of losing partner continuity while also incorporating an explicit ownership transfer. The recommendation is not to treat the handoff as a generic staffing sequence. It should be designed as a relationship transfer, with named decision rights at each stage and a visible transition-cost view.
package.recommendationRationale:
The strongest direct support for this position is the Executive Director’s statement that Bethany House cannot lose continuity with long-standing partners during a transition. The Operations Lead also indicated that a phased handoff is workable if ownership is explicit. Those two points make the phased handoff a strong fit for the current frame: it protects continuity while requiring named accountability rather than leaving responsibility informal.

The available record also suggests that the staffing gap may be showing up as a relationship-continuity problem, not only as a resourcing problem. Bethany House has partner relationships that cannot simply be moved as a contact list or task queue. A phased relationship handoff is better aligned with that risk than a purely structural role split or a temporary buffer, because it focuses on transferring partner history and decision ownership together.

The main contrary evidence is cost visibility. This option does not automatically make transition costs clear. Staged capacity can hide total cost through overlap time, training, duplicated effort, and leadership coaching unless Bethany House adds a simple cost view from the start. For that reason, the recommendation should include a transition budget or cost tracker covering overlap, onboarding, training time, and any duplicated responsibilities.
package.currentPositionStatement: Recommend that Bethany House pursue a phased relationship handoff: add capacity in stages while explicitly transferring partner history and decision ownership. The handoff should name the current owner, the incoming owner, the decision rights at each stage, the partner-history elements that must transfer, and the costs of overlap and support. This keeps the recommendation focused on continuity, not just capacity.

Without a specific partner handoff owner, relationship history could still be exposed during transition.

candidateCommentary.rationale: This remains a live option because it directly protects external relationship continuity during a capacity transition. package.closingNote: This decision should be reopened if any tripwire appears: partners do not accept the incoming owner, staff or partners keep escalating to the prior owner, decision rights remain unclear during overlap, leadership lacks time to transfer partner history, the phased plan becomes a delay mechanism, or overlap and training costs are not visible to leadership and the board. Those conditions would not automatically invalidate the recommendation, but they would require Bethany House to revisit the comparison before continuing the same path.

Bethany House Recommendation Brief
A decision position for discussion with Bethany House
Bethany House’s current decision frame centers on continuity with long-standing partners, explicit ownership during transition, and visibility into transition costs. Within that frame, the recommended position is: “Phased relationship handoff — Add capacity in stages while explicitly transferring partner history and decision ownership.”
Decision frame
The reply emphasizes continuity with long-standing partners, explicit ownership, and cost visibility during transition.
Recommendation
Phased relationship handoff
Add capacity in stages while explicitly transferring partner history and decision ownership.
The selected position is the comparison leader. It ranks ahead of “Separate operational and people ownership” and “Relationship continuity buffer” because it most directly addresses the stated risk of losing partner continuity while also incorporating an explicit ownership transfer. The recommendation is not to treat the handoff as a generic staffing sequence. It should be designed as a relationship transfer, with named decision rights at each stage and a visible transition-cost view.
The strongest direct support for this position is the Executive Director’s statement that Bethany House cannot lose continuity with long-standing partners during a transition. The Operations Lead also indicated that a phased handoff is workable if ownership is explicit. Those two points make the phased handoff a strong fit for the current frame: it protects continuity while requiring named accountability rather than leaving responsibility informal. The available record also suggests that the staffing gap may be showing up as a relationship-continuity problem, not only as a resourcing problem. Bethany House has partner relationships that cannot simply be moved as a contact list or task queue. A phased relationship handoff is better aligned with that risk than a purely structural role split or a temporary buffer, because it focuses on transferring partner history and decision ownership together. The main contrary evidence is cost visibility. This option does not automatically make transition costs clear. Staged capacity can hide total cost through overlap time, training, duplicated effort, and leadership coaching unless Bethany House adds a simple cost view from the start. For that reason, the recommendation should include a transition budget or cost tracker covering overlap, onboarding, training time, and any duplicated responsibilities.
Recommend that Bethany House pursue a phased relationship handoff: add capacity in stages while explicitly transferring partner history and decision ownership. The handoff should name the current owner, the incoming owner, the decision rights at each stage, the partner-history elements that must transfer, and the costs of overlap and support. This keeps the recommendation focused on continuity, not just capacity.

The case for a phased relationship handoff rests on three direct requirements in the current Bethany House record: capacity must be added, partner history must stay intact, and program leadership must own the handoff. This option addresses all three in its basic design. It also fits the relationship-continuity concern behind the capacity pressure: if one person is carrying too many partner relationships, the risk is not only workload but loss of context, trust, and decision memory during transfer.

The recommendation should therefore be framed as a structured handoff, not a loose transition. The phases need named owners, dates, exit conditions, and a shared record of partner history. Program leadership should own the handoff milestones and acceptance criteria from the start, not simply receive ownership after the relationship context has already been filtered elsewhere. The staged approach is valuable only if it creates usable capacity while protecting partner continuity.
